headline: Frank Little lynched in Butte, 1917
body:
The summer of 1917 in Butte, Montana, was a cauldron of industrial strife, a battleground where the raw power of capital clashed violently with the desperate cries of labor. Copper, the lifeblood of the Anaconda Copper Mining Company, was in unprecedented demand, fueling the war machine across the Atlantic. Yet, the men who toiled in the depths of the earth, extracting this vital metal, found their lives cheapened, their pleas for fair wages and safer conditions met with deaf ears and iron fists. Into this volatile arena stepped Frank Little, a fiery organizer for the Industrial Workers of the World, the Wobblies. He was a man of mixed heritage, part Cherokee, part white, but wholly dedicated to the cause of the working class. He arrived in Butte in July, his voice a clarion call for solidarity amidst the ongoing miners' strike, a strike that had brought the city's vital industry to a grinding halt.

Little's message was simple, yet revolutionary: "I stand for the solidarity of labor." He spoke against the war, against the exploitation of workers, and against the Anaconda Company's stranglehold on the lives of Butte's populace. His words, sharp and unyielding, resonated with the downtrodden miners, but they also ignited a furious hatred among the powerful elite. The company, accustomed to absolute control, saw in Little a dangerous agitator, a threat to their profits and their dominion. The local press, largely controlled by corporate interests, painted him as a seditious radical, an enemy of the state.

The night of July 31, 1917, was thick with menace. Six masked men, their identities shielded by the darkness and the complicity of power, stormed Little's boarding house. They dragged him from his bed, a sick man suffering from a broken ankle, and forced him into a waiting automobile. What followed was a brutal, agonizing journey. He was beaten, tortured, and tied to the bumper of the car, dragged for miles over the rough Montana terrain. The message, scrawled on a note pinned to his body, was chillingly clear: "Others take notice, first and last warning."

In the early hours of August 1, his broken body was found hanging from a railroad trestle on the outskirts of Butte, a grim tableau against the dawn sky. The lynching of Frank Little was not merely a murder; it was a calculated act of terror, a stark warning to any who dared to challenge the established order. The perpetrators were never brought to justice, their identities swallowed by the silence of a town gripped by fear and corporate power. Thousands attended Little's funeral, a silent, defiant procession that transformed into a powerful protest against the injustices that had claimed his life. His death became a symbol, a bloody stain on the fabric of American labor history, a testament to the brutal lengths to which capital would go to crush the spirit of solidarity. The copper continued to flow, the war raged on, but the echoes of Frank Little's sacrifice reverberated through the canyons of Butte, a haunting reminder of the price of dissent.
